intro:
A mountain (Hebrew har; Greek oros) is, in its concrete sense, the high ground that towers over the inhabited land — a place of refuge, of striking height, and of nearness to the sky. In the symbolic world of Scripture the mountain becomes the meeting-place of heaven and earth: the elevated ground where God descends and human beings ascend, where covenants are cut, worship is offered, and the kingdom is unveiled. From first page to last, decisive things happen on mountains.

The motif begins in Eden, which Ezekiel later remembers as "the holy mountain of God" (Ezekiel 28:14), a garden-sanctuary raised above the world. After the flood the ark comes to rest on the mountains of Ararat (Genesis 8:4), and Abraham is sent to offer Isaac on a mountain in Moriah, naming the place "The LORD will provide" (Genesis 22:2; Genesis 22:14). The pattern crystallizes at Sinai, where God descends in fire and cloud, the people are warned to keep their distance, and Moses ascends to receive the law and the pattern of the tabernacle (Exodus 19:18–20; Exodus 24:12). The mountain is holy because the LORD is there.

Sinai's holiness is then carried to Zion, the mountain God chooses for his dwelling and his King (Psalm 2:6; Psalm 48:1–2). The prophets look beyond the present to "the mountain of the house of the LORD" established as the highest of the mountains, to which all nations stream to learn his ways (Isaiah 2:2–3; Micah 4:1–2). On the LORD's mountain death itself will be swallowed up and the veil over the nations removed (Isaiah 25:6–8). Daniel sees a stone cut without hands strike the kingdoms of the world and become "a great mountain" that fills the earth — the everlasting kingdom of God (Daniel 2:35; Daniel 2:44).

In the Gospels Jesus gathers the mountain themes to himself. He teaches the new law from a mountain (Matthew 5:1), is transfigured in glory on a high mountain alongside Moses and Elijah (Matthew 17:1–2), prays alone on mountains, and from a mountain commissions his disciples to the nations (Matthew 28:16–18). His death and exaltation become the true Moriah and Zion. The book of Hebrews makes the contrast explicit: believers have not come to Sinai, the mountain of terror, but to "Mount Zion, the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem" (Hebrews 12:18–24).

The motif closes where it began, on a high mountain. John is carried away to see the holy city, new Jerusalem, descending from God — the eternal mountain-sanctuary where heaven and earth are joined forever and the Lamb is the temple (Revelation 21:10). The mountain matters because it traces the whole drama of access to God: once fenced with fire, now opened in Christ, and finally filling the renewed creation with his presence.
